title: Mumford—Tate and André—Oort Conjectures in Characteristic p
speaker: Ruofan Jiang
abstract: I will talk about mod p versions of the Mumford—Tate and André—Oort conjectures. Via a notion of formal linearity, the two conjectures, together with a third one (modpAx—Lindemann), are closely entangled with each other — much closer than their char 0 counterparts. This, for example, reduces the Mumford—Tate conjecture in char p to an “unlikely intersection problem”.  I will talk about my work on establishing the three conjectures for products of orthogonal Shimura varieties. If time permits, I will also go through more recent results beyond the orthogonal case.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: Classical Mumford--Tate benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the characteristic-zero Mumford--Tate group of an abelian variety, its comparison with ℓ-adic Galois monodromy, known cases, and its relation to the Hodge and Tate conjectures.

proof_stages.label: Ordinary mod-p Mumford--Tate group and conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Because Hodge structures are unavailable in characteristic p, the talk defines the mod-p Mumford--Tate group for ordinary abelian varieties or ordinary families via the smallest Shimura subvariety whose reduction contains the point or image, and formulates the analogous monodromy comparison conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Corrected geometric André--Oort formulation in characteristic p
proof_stages.summary: The naive CM-point version of André--Oort over an algebraic closure of a finite field is shown to fail, so the relevant geometric version uses positive-dimensional special subvarieties and concludes only the presence of a positive-dimensional special factor.

proof_stages.label: Serre--Tate formal linearity and mod-p Ax--Lindemann
proof_stages.summary: At an ordinary point the formal completion of the Shimura variety has a Serre--Tate formal-torus structure, special subvarieties are t-linear by Noot's theorem, and the mod-p Ax--Lindemann conjecture predicts that the smallest formal subtorus containing a completed subvariety algebraizes and is special.

proof_stages.label: Monodromy bridge via parabolicity and local monodromy
proof_stages.summary: Overconvergent F-isocrystals, their convergent restrictions, and their restrictions to formal completions have monodromy groups related by parabolicity and Chai's local monodromy theorem, identifying formal-linear directions with the relevant unipotent radical and enabling endomorphism and specialness arguments in GSpin/unitary cases.

proof_stages.label: Equivalence with mod-p Mumford--Tate and fourfold applications
proof_stages.summary: Using Mumford--Tate pairs together with parabolicity, companion theory, and t-linearity, the speaker states an equivalence between mod-p Ax--Lindemann and mod-p Mumford--Tate and sketches a resulting Mumford--Tate theorem for certain ordinary fourfold/K3-type situations via crystalline local systems, formal GAGA, and André--Oort.

proof_stages.label: Proof strategy for geometric mod-p André--Oort
proof_stages.summary: For a subvariety containing a dense collection of positive-dimensional special subvarieties, the completions of the special pieces form scaling-stable formal subschemes; Chai rigidity, global Serre--Tate coordinates, Tannakian monodromy, and Mumford--Tate rank estimates force t-linearity and hence specialness or a special factor.

examples.name: Nonspecial curve times a modular curve in a triple product
examples.note: In a triple product of modular curves, taking a nonspecial curve in two factors and multiplying by the third modular curve gives many positive-dimensional special subvarieties but is not itself special, forcing the geometric André--Oort conclusion to be weakened to existence of a special factor.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the triple-product example, draws it, and checks why the curve-times-modular-curve subvariety contains many special subvarieties but is not itself special
